quirck: Not much to comment yet, all moves are pretty standard. I got a base so that black can't build a moyo on the top. The k17 group can't be considered 100% safe, but for now it's ok.

quirck: Quite expectable. I think i won't go for something fancy like pincers. Two reasons here: my favorite sequence (see above) ends in gote, so I won't have time to place k4. Second, some pros teach that pincer is good when it has support from the neighbour corner. In this case, q16 is black, and the support is from d4. So answer is better. So o3
quirck: If black goes for standard joseki
quirck: he will be low, and r9-q16 pair won't work efficiently. But if black replies at q10, i may have to worry about right side moyo.

C[quirck: Interestingly, was it misclick, or you changed your mind..
C[quirck: r13 is a bit inconsistent with r17. While r17 defends the corner and aims on territory, r13 attacks the group but leaves the corner in danger

quirck: Move 14: I don't know any joseki with this move
quirck: And it was for this reason I replied to c4 with c5
quirck: As for c3, with hoshi d4 i from the beginning aim more to influence than for territory. I may willingly give you the corner building wall facing k4 in return
quirck: The correct way to stop white depends on the presence of black stone at c13
quirck: If there is no stone, like in our game, then
quirck: Sorry, I meant c12, not c13 :)
quirck: If c12 is black, then
quirck: white can't go further to a14 since black cuts at a12
quirck: Actually you might want to read http://senseis.xmp.net/?Monkey and http://senseis.xmp.net/?MonkeyJump%2FIntoHighPosition on this topic
quirck: Well, that's it for the time being. Hope it helps :)
wiking: Thanks for your comments and thoughts. There were two or three undos on my behalf all from slips of fingers. I never ask for undo when I realize I made a mistake. That's like cheating.
wiking: I don't know any josekis. You think I should study them?

quirck: Well, it may be fun to learn through playing, and this way you may even discover something more powerful than existing joseki variants. Or confuse your opponent with unexpected move, thus putting him into uncomfortable position where he is likely to make mistakes... Sounds like fun. As for me, I learnt about 5-6 joseki from the beginning, and then, every time i was confronted with an unknown response, i went and looked up the standard sequence. This way i picked up about 10 more josekis, but still i often forget some moves. So I won't give any recommendation :) Usually people say that you should _study_ joseki, not _learn_ them by heart. So maybe it'll be helpful to take joseki as a way to just look up how to play in certain situations, that is, to see what is considered best play nowadays.

quirck: I was a bit surprised with the upper right corner.. Do you know eye shapes? They're definitely worth learning as they have been thoroughly studied (unlike joseki) and save a great deal of reading:)
wiking: I guess I was desperately hoping you would make a mistake in that corner.
quirck: Ah :)) That happens, I've recently overlooked a fatal atari on my huge group :D I was too lazy to count dame, and I paid for it :D
